names.japanese_name: エチルメチルセルロース
names.japanese_label_name: エチルメチルセルロース

natural_occurrence.description: Ethyl methyl cellulose is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose, created by chemically modifying plant cellulose with both ethyl and methyl groups. It's processed wood fiber or cotton that has been chemically altered.

manufacturing.description: Produced by treating alkali cellulose with a mixture of methyl chloride and ethyl chloride, substituting hydroxyl groups with both methyl and ethyl ether groups. The dual substitution creates unique properties intermediate between methyl cellulose and ethyl cellulose.

other_uses.cosmetics: Film-former and thickener in cosmetics and personal care products.
other_uses.household: Less common than other cellulose ethers.

eu.conditions: Approved for use in specified food categories; less commonly used than other cellulose derivatives.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: エチルメチルセルロース

commonly_found_in: Less commonly used in foods compared to other cellulose derivatives
Some processed foods as thickener and stabilizer
fun_facts: Like all cellulose derivatives, it's chemically modified wood pulp or plant fiber - literally processed sawdust!
Ethyl methyl cellulose combines properties of both methyl cellulose (water-soluble) and ethyl cellulose (water-resistant)
It's particularly good at creating stable foams, which is why it's used in whipped toppings and aerated products
Completely indigestible by humans, so it passes through as zero-calorie dietary fiber
Less common in food use than its cousin HPMC (E464), which has largely replaced it in many applications
